speaker: interviewer
text: Lothar Singer in London, England. We've reached the middle of-- of 1940. After the-- did you have a summer vacation that year, from your studies.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not. I came on the 27th of June back from my unit. And the night of the 28th, the Red Army entered the town. That was finish of my Romanian officer's career. There were the Romanians [? started ?] retreat, units, military units. I saw them retreating. The mob started looting, Ukrainian in particular, because we had a large population. That was a-- whole transfer was a peaceful transfer. It was in the base of the Hitler, Ribbentrop, Molotov Pact. The Bucovina-- the northern part of the Bucovina became Soviet, and Bessarabia. So the Romania had to go out. And that's who was in the hands of Hitler. We stood on, the Jewish, only a small part-- the very, very rich-- risk running. Some were afterwards beaten, and looted on the way. But there started coming a lot of Jews from Romania, which were now under Red Guard-- a Red Guard government took over. And Hitler's emissaries were sitting in Bucharest. So some hope they will escape and come to-- over to the Russian occupied part.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Romania at this time was split in a similar way to-- to Poland then. Partly Russian occupation, partly-- I mean the Germans weren't actually in Romania.

speaker: interviewee
text: The German were in independent Romania, if you could call it so. In the Russian occupied territory were Bessarabia and the northern part of Bucovina, my province where I lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: About what proportion of Romania was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It were about 20% of the territory.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was the Russian administration efficient?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, the-- this was a local administration, which continued. Local people. You see the member of the Communist Party took over. The member Luca, he was many, many years in jail, a leader of the underground Communist Party. He took over. As a matter of fact, once he came in, I went to the-- next day to the town hall. There the authority [RUSSIAN] is the Russian officer. I didn't know who this was-- a higher officer. And he asked me for coins, in German. We were speaking German. Coins-- if I have. I had some Romanian coins and I offered them. And he's took out a piece of bread-- that rye bread which the army had. And he gave it to introduce himself General Zhukov. He was a commanding officer of the troop occupying the town. Afterwards, he was very famous as Marshall Zhukov. I met him later in Germany, and-- and he still remembered this episode.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the food situation?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- they're preparing for-- for such a thing. In the town, as I have told you, had the rich agricultural hinterland. So the people still continued bringing in food. The town population bought the food, and the shops were still there where [? the ?] people had [? brought ?] to buy. But [? it was ?] the Russian came, the exchange rate with the food was very high.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they started emptying the shelves. They found things which they'd never had there. And if you ask them, oh, do you have this? Of course. Do you have lemons? Can you get lemons? Oh yes. We have whole factories which may produce. And it was a very afraid of starting friendship with the local population.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they were not prohibited from starting friendships but were told that perhaps some capitalist would poison them-- stories. But afterwards, they got more pleasant, except as they needed quarters, living quarters, for their families [? abroad ?] [INAUDIBLE] through the local population [INAUDIBLE]. Wasn't enough for the Romanian have left, and this even to the nice Jewish flats or houses. There started to be problems. We were under-- I still was in the university up to Erev Yom Kippur. It was in-- it was that end of September, beginning of October. I couldn't tell you exactly. And they stopped for the time being the mathematic physics department because they had not enough student. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But who doesn't work doesn't eat. So you have to go the labor exchange, [RUSSIAN] as the Russian called it, and to ask for work. I went, and they sent me to the beer brewery to work clerical, chemist, statistician. When I came there, it was in October. And they were preparing for the 11th of November, since the 11th was the national day of the Soviet Union, Revolution Day. And they were preparing, so somebody had to-- to write on the red cloth the payrolls which were prepared.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, I could do this. And I started this and gentleman from the upper [? thousandth ?] of the comrades here, he inspected, and he was very interested what I was doing, what I know. And I said, oh, you are mathematicians? It means you can count. He has supervising the activities of the state bank. Said, look, I give you the choice. Or you go in finance service, or you go in the [? cost ?] bank.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Cost ?] bank is not a bank in the-- like we see it here in the capitalist system. It is a [? higher ?] control system. The factories, the stores have only what we call petty cash. All the money has to be put in the bank, their account. The bank pays for the wares when they come in. And it pays the salaries, and everything has to go through the bank. The raw material for factories, they have to come visit bank to get a permission to order it. The bank pays for you. And when it's ready, you-- gives the finished production, you sent to the cooperative stores. And they pay through the bank. So as nobody has-- in addition to the petty cash, nobody has money.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your job was to what?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- we had to study. So they made-- because they needed staff, so they made a short course from the different university to train us. I was from the institute [? cost ?] bank, [INAUDIBLE] state bank institute that was a branch. We got teachers from Odessa and Moscow from the state bank institute, which taught us-- it was three months. And then we finished-- I finished also one of the top and our-- it is called the English name, the chairman, director general, was Bulganin, which afterwards became Marshall Bulganin. And the government [INAUDIBLE] the government. He presented me with my diploma, which it gave me some priorities. I could choose where I want to work. And I got 2,000 ruble, a price. In order to give you an example, it was 2,000 ruble. My father, as director of the beer brewery, [INAUDIBLE] director, got 400 ruble a month. I got a surprise 2,000 ruble, plus all the volumes of Lenin, plus all the volume of Stalin, which were-- I wouldn't have been able to read them all. They were good for [? tie ?] present.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were instructed in Russian, and you-- you were fluent in Russian at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not. I did not know. I could speak Ukrainian. I could speak because I had servants. I knew the Cyrillic language from the school, because the old Romanian was written in Cyrillic. And being a so-called church school, they learned the old saltier, to read the saltier. So I learned it also. But I did not know Russian. My grandfather found the old, from the Tsarist time, dictionary, [? produced ?] dictionary. And I then, by hearing, and combining phrases in mathematics, [INAUDIBLE] [? made ?] economics, I knew what it is in German or in Romanian. And I once a second time. And a certainty I learned it, the hard way, but I learned. In three months, in March, I finished. And I was-- I choose to go to Hlyboka or [? Gluboka ?] in the Russian that was a bank on the border with Romania. There we didn't exchange many ruble too because to-- as there were some Englishmen, some Americans who came in. But it was a local bank really.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the official Russian attitude to Jews at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had many Jews in high positions, both in the civil authority-- Russian Jews, Ukrainian Jews from the Soviet Union, some of them high member in the Party. And the officials were not permitted to show anti-Semitism, but it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they close the synagogues in Romania?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at this-- not at this time. It was later to come.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or the churches?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not. Not at this time. They let still open. They didn't have the time, you see, in-- the war started. And the 22nd of June, 1941 the war started with Germany. The war caught me on my way to my home town from [? Gluboka ?] where I worked. I was good with my boss, my director. So I-- I was officially permitted to go. I had a pass. In Russia, you couldn't go from one place to the other without permission. I was good with my director, and he gave me to bring stationery from my home town, from Cernauti, where the higher organization of the state bank was. So I could, every week, go home. I brought my parents agricultural food from the peasant, because in town, you couldn't get butter or-- or porridge [? out. ?] So I brought it from there. And from the town I could get for me what I needed. My mother was preparing food for me, and [? so ?] I took it to-- I lived with a Jewish family in [? Gluboka ?] and worked as senior [ROMANIAN], a senior accountant in the local bank as I said.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you travel between [? Gluboka ?] and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Train.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take then?

speaker: interviewee
text: About half an hour. I was caught by the German Luftwaffe in the train. We didn't know that the war started. And I was on the way to my home town, nearly in my town, when they bombed the local airport [INAUDIBLE]. I ran home. My parents were still in the bed on Sunday morning. And I said, war has started. They didn't-- couldn't believe, but they saw there were some Russian officers which lived in the same house. And they ran-- they all ran into their unit. It was a [? coward ?] situation. I could not go back to my bank, was stopped. So I went to the headquarter of the banking and announced. So they said, OK, we'll mobilize yourself. I get-- I got the first lieutenant, the lowest rank, officer rank-- and to go bank-- your bank is a field bank now. There was sent the uniform, [INAUDIBLE] uniform was sent back to [INAUDIBLE] to the-- my bank. I had to walk, of course, back. And it was about 20 kilometer. The other [INAUDIBLE] of my [? region ?] were teachers, and they're living together. [INAUDIBLE] it-- it's-- we were there up to the 1st of June-- 1st or 2nd of June-- 1st of June. And then they started their retreat. I retreated with the army and with the old Russian officials, the local population some. I retreated through my home town. I couldn't leave because my director and the other higher in rank and the credit inspector in the [INAUDIBLE] army. Had to join the army. I was slept with the other five and the money. And we went the direction, were directed in direction of [INAUDIBLE]. That is on the [NON-ENGLISH]. I we crossed there in [NON-ENGLISH]. There were peasants, which were forced to, with their carriages, to bring us on. We crossed the [NON-ENGLISH]. It was night. During the day they-- usually the German bomb, the Stukas, across in the night of [INAUDIBLE] and continued. And there were some Russian soldiers which directed us in this direction to go. We didn't realize. Suddenly, I recognized their German uniforms. We enter and the Russians are encircled-- the German encircled us in their tank units. And the-- the so-called Russian guides, they're German in Russian uniforms. Immediately, they closed and said in rows to stay. And it was immediately two hours of this. They're standing not knowing what to do. Yes, in the October war, which created, I set fire to the money. [INAUDIBLE] not to [INAUDIBLE] the enemy. But I was in [INAUDIBLE] to escape. It was still dark, and I heard the shout, [? commissary, ?] commissars and Jews [GERMAN] to come forward. I say, it's not for me. So I tried, and I succeeded in escaping in the direction to the west I was marching. I succeeded crossing the [? Niesse ?] in the other direction. I got rid of my uniform, hid as a peasant. And a lot of peasant then driven by the German over to the west for forced labor. When I came in the other side, I knew more or less the region, and knew my direction, tried to get home. I came home in the same day as the German. They first encircled the town, and then they took it. German and Romanian troops entered. I came back as [INAUDIBLE] for son, dirty, as a peasant boy, came to my parents. And the girls were here. A mob started looting and killing and this. In the first week, 3,000 people, mostly Jews, were killed. The [INAUDIBLE] then, our chief Rabbi, the mostly intelligentsia they picked out. They checked on the hands. If you did not have working hands, so this proves you were intelligentsia. And they would take you out. Many of my friends and acquaintances were killed in the mass grave near the [INAUDIBLE] road. Afterward they went cemetery. That was the begin. I personally was with my parents and a cousin of mine, which was in the other town. And he came and stood with us. His family was in South Bucovina. He stood with us. It was about 12 o'clock during the day, knocking on the door, soldiers-- out, out, out! The [INAUDIBLE] said somebody had shot on the victorious Romanian and German troops and killed one officer. Two, three, five-- they took us out for shooting in front of the house. My entire family-- parents, my sister, my cousin-- all of them [INAUDIBLE] as they're mostly Jews. The [INAUDIBLE] [? boss, ?] my father started speaking to them. The one German officer, which he spoke in German to him, told him I am a holder of the Iron Cross and so-and-so. But our luck, there came a higher Romanian officer and stopped a priest, a field chaplain, which was inciting them to shoot us.

speaker: interviewee
text: But this higher officer kept on-- Major, I couldn't-- I don't remember his name. Stop them. [? He said, ?] how did they shoot? You see the windows are closed. And they say, oh, these Jews shoot like this. But he saw that it's only-- I didn't know-- don't know [? or ?] he has pity, or he was not so anti-Jewish. He let us go. That was the first encounter. We invited the Romanian officer to stay with us. He stood with us, [? Beckman. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: And that saved us for [INAUDIBLE] dark and for looting until the last day of Sukkot. It was the 11th of October. In the meantime, we were taken, of course, to forced labor. The bridges had to be rebuilt, and [? rebuilt ?] all of us the houses which the Russian destroyed, in the retreat. So we were put to work. Many were killed. They're too lazy for the German, so they were killed, by Romanian and the German. I worked also forced labor. My late father, who had a permanent position in the Old Veterans Association, was taken as a hostage. In case something happens, he will be shot. [? 100 ?] Jews. There was one or two Ukrainian, but mostly they're Jews. It was a month, the houses and the Romanian barracks, military barracks. Then they put me for two weeks in. And there we-- my mother brought food, because they didn't supply us with food. [INAUDIBLE] [? families ?] [? were ?] permitted to-- to move out to the [? pressing, ?] but it was also paid by the Jewish community. And they did not really beat us too much. Some were badly beaten, but no one was shot. And we are all the time under danger of being shot if something happens. You didn't know what can happen. Then I was let out and had to work [INAUDIBLE] with the young people until the-- it was in a Saturday they made an agreement with the German [? pressed ?] to send us off. And we had in the town an Einsatzgruppen, [? Oldenburg. ?] They were the [? Obersturm ?] [INAUDIBLE] the boss was [? Oldenburg. ?] And we had [? 10B ?] was the one who executed the people in this. They were not pleasant. They occupied the central hotel, where the execution were usually in the lift. And then, you had to take them out, or they threw them out in the front. I remember like the day and they're standing there, the SS guard with his medal. It was not very pleasant. The [? beatings ?] where some officer, which [? shows ?] [? their ?] [INAUDIBLE] [? guard ?] this member was wearing uniform now. And it was a hard time. It was very early in the morning. We were not permitted to go out after 7 o'clock, if I remember well, for dark. It was early morning. My aunt, the younger sister of my mother, came running. You are still home. You don't know. Pack quickly. Ordered to go in the ghetto. They instituted the ghetto, a few streets. This is surrounded with barbed wires, and they made a gate. All had to be until 5 o'clock, 6 o'clock-- I don't remember, but in the evening to move in the ghetto-- the entire Jewish population. You had about 40,000 Jews in the town-- 30,000 Jews in the town. But there were many Jews, which came from the surrounding-- which survived in the surrounding small townships and villages [? that ?] came all to the town. We came to my aunt, one of my aunt, which lived in this city. She has a large flat. So we were my grandfather, my step grandmother, my eldest uncle with his five married [? son ?] with the wife, the younger son. It was our-- our family, we four, a cousin. We were full there. We slept out on the floor, of course. And we [? what ?]had we had. Because when we went, we took what we could take on the-- we were permitted only entering to the ghetto-- not to go out again. We saved what we could save, food and this, and we brought it in the ghetto. There you could move. You [? really ?] had to give up the keys with the address [INAUDIBLE] of your flat. It all was taken away. You can imagine my parents, after so many years. They married in 1919. The had to go out of their home, which they had all collected and [? homeless, ?] and this for [? nothing ?] with a-- a rucksack-- not a rucksack, a bag. And we stood there. Then came the order of deportation. Officially, it was for colonization Ukraine. They told now population [? the ?] Ukraine, the Russian have taken off. You will have to colonize and work there, agriculture. All right. And the SS started [? commanding ?] [? around. ?] They were SS officer and Gestapo. We had to go ourselves. And there were street after street, and they were always reducing the area of the ghetto. You had to walk yourself to the [? umliegeplatz. ?] It was in the station. They had put us in trains, not [INAUDIBLE] only. They were [? good stays. ?] The windows, the small windows, were barred with barbed wire. And they put the Jews in. You had to give up your jewelry, your wedding rings, everything of value.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in the ghetto before this happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: From 11th of October until about 14, 12, 14th of November.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in 1941?

speaker: interviewee
text: '41-- short time only.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're coming to the end of this tape now.
